pr.title: More pointers converted to labels and some ASCII cleanup
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: Converted many raw address literals in assembly data sections into symbolic labels and cleaned up ASCII data that had been represented as hexadecimal .4byte words. The PR mostly touched asm/data.s, with supporting label boundary additions in asm/bss.s and asm/sdata.s, making pointer tables and embedded strings easier to read and safer to reference during future decompilation.

key_files.role: Main data cleanup file; many raw .4byte address constants were replaced with lbl_* references and several hex-encoded ASCII sequences were rewritten as .asciz strings with labels.
Small-data section cleanup; added many labels and converted raw pointer constants where applicable, though the provided diff excerpt is limited.
Split large .skip ranges into smaller labeled regions so existing raw BSS pointers could be referenced as labels.

what_changed: Added explicit labels in asm/bss.s for previously unlabeled subregions, including lbl_80459C10, lbl_80459CA0, lbl_80459D18, lbl_80459D90, lbl_80459DF0, lbl_804D68E0, lbl_804D6908, and lbl_804D6DD4.
Replaced raw BSS address constants in asm/data.s with labels, for example .4byte 0x80459CA0 became .4byte lbl_80459CA0 and .4byte 0x804D68E0 became .4byte lbl_804D68E0.

Converted a large block of hex-encoded movie-end filename strings into readable .asciz entries, such as MvEndCaptain.mth, MvEndDonkey.mth, MvEndFox.mth, MvEndGamewatch.mth, and related character-specific files.
Added labels for those newly readable strings, including lbl_803DB050 through lbl_803DB1E4, then changed the following pointer table to refer to those labels instead of raw 0x803DBxxx addresses.

Added labels inside larger data structures where later entries pointed into the middle of the structure, such as lbl_803DD0C8, lbl_803DD0DC, lbl_803E0D74, lbl_803E0E50, lbl_803E0F0C, lbl_803E0F20, lbl_803E0FF4, lbl_803E1198, lbl_803E126C, lbl_803E14D4, lbl_803E14E8, lbl_803E1704, lbl_803E17F4, lbl_803E1A30, lbl_803E1B20, lbl_803E1D8C, and lbl_803E2740.
Converted additional asset filename text from hex .4byte sequences to .asciz, including GrPs1.dat, GrPs2.dat, GrPs3.dat, and GrPs4.dat.
Preserved alignment after string conversion using .balign 4 so the subsequent data and pointer table layout remained consistent.
The PR changed only assembly data-section files: asm/data.s, asm/sdata.s, and asm/bss.s.
smart_moves: Split .skip blocks in bss.s before converting pointers, ensuring each newly symbolic pointer had an actual label at the referenced address instead of pointing into an anonymous allocation range.
Converted both the pointed-to data and the pointer table together: for the MvEnd*.mth strings, the PR introduced labels on the strings and then replaced table entries with those labels.
Used .asciz plus .balign 4 for ASCII cleanup, improving readability while preserving null termination and 4-byte alignment expected by neighboring .4byte data.
Kept generic lbl_ADDRESS naming for newly identified locations rather than inventing semantic names unsupported by the evidence.
Converted raw addresses only where a matching label was introduced or already existed, reducing relocation ambiguity in data tables.
decomp_lessons: When a raw .4byte address targets the middle of a data or BSS range, first split the range and add a label at the exact target address; then replace the literal with the label.
Hex words that decode to printable null-terminated strings should be rewritten as .asciz when doing so preserves alignment and byte layout.
Pointer tables become much more searchable and less error-prone when entries use labels rather than absolute addresses.
For data-only cleanup PRs, readability gains can be large even without matching C functions; these changes help later struct recovery and asset table identification.
Do not assign semantic symbol names prematurely; this PR retained address-style lbl_* labels where stronger naming evidence was not present.
naming_conventions: New anonymous symbols used the project’s address-based label format, e.g. lbl_803DB050, lbl_803E15F0, lbl_80459CA0.
Global labels were declared with a .global directive immediately before the label definition.
Readable embedded filenames used their literal asset casing in .asciz, for example "MvEndCaptain.mth", "MvEndGamewatch.mth", and "GrPs1.dat".
No semantic C-style variable names were introduced in the shown diff; labels remained address-derived.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace `.4byte 0xADDR` with `.4byte lbl_ADDR` after ensuring `lbl_ADDR` exists at the exact location.
For BSS, split a single `.skip` into multiple `.skip` segments separated by labels to expose internal pointer targets without changing total size.
For ASCII cleanup, replace big-endian hex `.4byte` sequences with `.asciz "..."` and add `.balign 4` to maintain word alignment.
When a data structure contains self-references or subtable references, label the subtable start and update all raw pointers to symbolic references.
Maintain duplicate pointer semantics explicitly: the MvEnd pointer table still contains two entries pointing at lbl_803DB180, matching the prior duplicate 0x803DB180 entries.
searchable_terms: More pointers converted to labels and some ASCII cleanup
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that convert these lbl_* data tables into named C structs or typed arrays.
Search for remaining raw .4byte 0x803D or 0x804D pointer constants in asm/data.s and asm/sdata.s.
Search for hex-encoded printable ASCII sequences still represented as .4byte words.
Check whether the MvEnd*.mth pointer table later receives a semantic symbol name.
Review later ground/stage data cleanup around GrPs*.dat and related labels such as lbl_803E15F0 and lbl_803E2740.
